project_identification.lead_agency: City of Riverbend Community Development Department

project_location.cross_streets: W. Bridge Street and Garden Avenue
project_location.within_2_miles_schools: Riverbend High School; Franklin Elementary School; Riverbend Community College Center

development_type.development_type_transportation_type: On-site circulation improvements, new signalized intersection, Class II and Class IV bicycle facilities, enhanced pedestrian crossings, and a transit stop with shelter along W. Bridge Street.
development_type.development_type_other: Public plaza, riverfront promenade, and neighborhood park facilities (approximately 2.9 acres of public open space).

project_issues_discussed_in_document.project_issues_other: Energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions; climate change resilience and heat-island effects.

existing_conditions_and_project_descript.present_land_use_zoning_general_plan_des:
Present land use: Largely vacant former industrial and warehouse area with scattered storage yards, a decommissioned fuel distribution facility, and an inactive lumber yard; several parcels are currently used for informal parking and equipment storage.

Zoning: M-1 (Light Industrial) and C-2 (General Commercial) with an overlay for the former Riverfront Industrial District.

General Plan designation: Employment Center and General Commercial with portions designated as Riverfront Transition. The project includes amendments to re-designate the site to Mixed-Use Riverfront and Neighborhood Commercial and to apply a Planned Development (PD) overlay to support a walkable, transit-supportive mixed-use district.
existing_conditions_and_project_descript.project_description:
The Riverbend Station Mixed-Use Redevelopment Project proposes to transform approximately 18.6 acres of underutilized, largely vacant former industrial land along the Feather River into a compact, walkable, transit-oriented neighborhood that integrates housing, neighborhood-serving retail, office space, and public open space.

Project Components
1. Residential Development
- Total units: 248 multi-family residential units in a mix of 3- to 5-story buildings.
- Unit mix (approximate): 40 studios, 110 one-bedroom units, 80 two-bedroom units, and 18 three-bedroom units.
- Affordability: At least 15% (approximately 37 units) reserved as deed-restricted affordable units targeted to low- and moderate-income households, with a focus on workforce housing for teachers, healthcare workers, and public-sector employees.
- Amenities: Shared courtyards, rooftop decks, indoor community rooms, a small co-working lounge for residents, secure bicycle storage, and ground-floor fitness and mail/package facilities.

2. Commercial and Office Uses
- Retail/restaurant: Approximately 58,000 square feet of ground-floor neighborhood-serving commercial space, including a small-format grocery, cafés, casual dining, local service businesses (e.g., dry cleaner, salon, bank/credit union), and potential maker/retail hybrid spaces oriented toward the public plaza and riverfront promenade.
- Office: Approximately 42,000 square feet of flexible office space on upper floors, intended for professional services, small tech and design firms, and non-profit organizations. The office space is anticipated to support approximately 165 employees at full occupancy.
- Design: Storefronts will be oriented toward W. Bridge Street, the internal main street, and the public plaza, with large windows, outdoor seating areas, and pedestrian-scaled lighting.

3. Public Open Space and Recreation
- Riverfront Promenade: A publicly accessible promenade running along the Feather River edge of the site, including a multi-use path, seating nodes, interpretive signage about local ecology and history, and overlooks with railings designed to meet safety standards.
- Central Plaza: A roughly 0.8-acre hard- and soft-scape public plaza designed to accommodate small community events, farmers markets, and outdoor performances. The plaza will include shade trees, movable seating, integrated public art, and a small splash pad or interactive water feature (non-contact, recirculating system) subject to water-conservation requirements.
- Neighborhood Park: Approximately 1.4 acres of neighborhood park space with a children’s play area, open lawn, half-court basketball or multi-use sport area, picnic tables, and a small dog relief area.
- Total public open space: Approximately 2.9 acres, including paseo connections and smaller pocket spaces.

4. Circulation, Access, and Parking
- Primary Vehicular Access: The main access will be from W. Bridge Street via a new signalized intersection aligned with Garden Avenue. A secondary right-in/right-out access will be provided at a driveway approximately 400 feet east of the new signal.
- Internal Street Network: A new internal “main street” will run north–south through the site, designed as a low-speed, pedestrian-priority street with on-street parking, street trees, bulb-outs, and enhanced crosswalks. Additional local drives and alleys will provide access to parking garages and loading areas.
- Bicycle and Pedestrian Facilities: The project will construct new Class II bike lanes along the project’s W. Bridge Street frontage and a Class IV separated bikeway along the internal main street. Sidewalks will be widened to 8–12 feet along key frontages, with Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA)-compliant curb ramps, tactile paving, and pedestrian-scale lighting.
- Transit: A new bus stop with shelter, seating, and real-time information display will be installed along W. Bridge Street in coordination with the Riverbend Transit District. The project is within a 10-minute walk of the existing downtown transit center.
- Parking: Approximately 520 parking spaces distributed among two wrapped parking structures, limited surface parking, and on-street spaces. Parking will be "unbundled" for residential units where feasible, and the project will implement transportation demand management (TDM) strategies such as transit pass subsidies for residents and employees and secure bicycle parking.

5. Infrastructure and Utilities
- Water: The project will connect to the City’s municipal water system, with new 8- and 12-inch water lines looped through the site for redundancy and fire flow. Water-efficient fixtures and drought-tolerant landscaping will be used throughout the project.
- Wastewater: Sewer flows will be conveyed to the City’s existing wastewater collection system via a new gravity main in Garden Avenue, sized to accommodate project buildout. Capacity analysis will confirm that downstream trunk lines and the treatment plant have adequate capacity; if needed, the developer will contribute to targeted improvements.
- Stormwater: The project will implement a low-impact development (LID) approach, including bioretention planters, permeable paving in selected parking and plaza areas, and underground detention facilities to maintain post-project runoff at or below pre-project levels. Stormwater quality treatment will target pollutants of concern such as sediment, trash, nutrients, and metals before discharge to the Feather River.
- Dry Utilities: Upgraded electric, gas, telecommunications, and fiber-optic infrastructure will be installed underground where feasible. The project will incorporate rooftop solar photovoltaic arrays on several buildings to offset a portion of common-area electricity use.

6. Environmental Remediation and Site Preparation
The site contains several legacy industrial uses, including a decommissioned bulk fuel storage facility and former lumber yard. Previous Phase I and Phase II environmental site assessments identified localized soil and potential groundwater contamination (primarily petroleum hydrocarbons and minor solvents) in limited areas.

Prior to construction, the project sponsor will work under the oversight of the Regional Water Quality Control Board and/or the Department of Toxic Substances Control, as appropriate, to implement a remediation and soil management plan. Anticipated measures include targeted excavation and off-site disposal of impacted soil, on-site stabilization where appropriate, installation of vapor barriers beneath residential and commercial buildings, and construction worker health and safety protocols.

7. Phasing
The project is anticipated to be constructed in three primary phases over approximately 6–8 years, depending on market conditions:
- Phase 1: Site remediation, backbone infrastructure, the first mixed-use residential building (approximately 90 units with ground-floor retail), a portion of the office space, and initial segments of the riverfront promenade and central plaza.
- Phase 2: Additional residential buildings (approximately 100 units), remaining core commercial uses including the small-format grocery, and completion of the central plaza and neighborhood park.
- Phase 3: Remaining residential units (approximately 58 units), final office space, buildout of the riverfront promenade, and any remaining internal street and utility improvements.

8. Objectives
Key project objectives include:
- Revitalize an underutilized former industrial area near downtown Riverbend with a high-quality, mixed-use development that supports the City’s long-term growth and housing goals.
- Provide a range of housing types and price points, including deed-restricted affordable units, to help address regional housing needs and reduce pressure for greenfield development at the City’s edges.
- Enhance public access to the Feather River by creating a continuous riverfront promenade and multiple view corridors from W. Bridge Street and the surrounding neighborhood.
- Support multi-modal transportation by locating higher-density development near existing transit and providing improved bicycle and pedestrian infrastructure.
- Remediate legacy contamination in a manner that protects public health and the environment and allows safe, long-term residential and commercial use of the site.

9. Surrounding Land Uses
- North: W. Bridge Street and, across the street, a mix of older one- and two-story commercial buildings and surface parking lots, gradually transitioning to the historic downtown core.
- South: The Feather River, associated riparian habitat, and an informal dirt path used by local residents for walking and fishing access.
- East: Existing light industrial and warehouse uses, some of which may transition over time to more intensive commercial or mixed-use consistent with the City’s long-range plans.
- West: A mix of older single-family homes, small apartment buildings, and neighborhood-scale retail uses along Garden Avenue.

The Mitigated Negative Declaration analyzes potential environmental impacts associated with the project, identifies feasible mitigation measures, and incorporates a comprehensive mitigation monitoring and reporting program to ensure that required measures are implemented during construction and operation.
